ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for braised beef noodles; FOODON_03315671 (noodle dish) used as the closest parent class.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a dedicated code for braised beef noodles; A0FJP (noodle-based dish with meat) used as the closest parent analogue.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT food concept maps specifically to braised beef noodles as of 2024.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard bowl of braised beef noodles weighs approximately 600 g and is the typical single-serve portion as listed on the GrabFood menu for Hock Kee Braised Beef Noodles.

nutrient_source.title: Health Promotion Board Singapore — Nutrient Composition of Food (HPB Foodo / Energy and Nutrient Composition of Food)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single HPB entry exists for 'Braised Beef Noodles' as a composite dish. Values are adapted from HPB Singapore nutrient data for beef noodle soup and component ingredients (wheat noodles, braised beef, soy-based broth) and cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for beef shank (USDA #23065) and wheat noodles (USDA #20113). Per-100g figures represent a weighted composite of approximately 25% beef shank, 35% wheat noodles, and 40% broth with vegetables, consistent with the input record values which are retained as they fall within the plausible range for this dish type.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for braised beef noodles as a composite dish. GI of 52 is estimated using wheat noodles (boiled, GI ~52 per University of Sydney GI Database, Atkinson et al. 2008) as the primary carbohydrate driver. The presence of protein (7.5 g/100g) and fat (4.17 g/100g) in the dish further attenuates the postprandial glucose response, making 52 a conservative upper-bound estimate. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (52 × 60.0) / 100 = 31.2 (carbohydrate per 600g serving = 10.0 × 6 = 60.0 g).

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 300 mg per 100 g; a full 600 g bowl delivers approximately 1800 mg sodium, representing 78–90% of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2000–2300 mg.
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should request reduced soy sauce and doubanjiang, avoid consuming the full broth, and limit portion size to no more than half a bowl to keep sodium intake below 900 mg per meal.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Carbohydrate 10 g per 100 g (60 g per full bowl) with an estimated GI of 52 yields a glycemic load of approximately 31 per serving, which is high; however, co-ingested protein (7.5 g/100g) and fat (4.17 g/100g) attenuate the postprandial glucose rise.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Patients with type 2 diabetes should request a reduced noodle portion (half serving) to lower glycemic load to approximately 15, prioritise the beef and vegetable components, and monitor postprandial glucose as the high-sodium broth may also contribute to insulin resistance over time.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Sodium is high at 300 mg/100g (1800 mg per bowl); saturated fat is moderate at 1.67 g/100g (10 g per bowl); trans fat is present at 0.08 g/100g (0.48 g per bowl).
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should limit this dish to occasional consumption, request reduced soy sauce and doubanjiang to lower sodium, avoid drinking the broth, and choose a smaller portion to limit cumulative saturated fat and trans fat intake.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Beef shank is a red meat rich in arachidonic acid (omega-6), which amplifies pro-inflammatory eicosanoid pathways; high sodium may worsen RA-related tissue oedema.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis should limit the beef portion, request omission of doubanjiang if sensitive to nightshades, and avoid consuming the full broth to reduce sodium-driven tissue swelling.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Braised Beef Noodles (红烧牛肉面, Hóng Shāo Niú Ròu Miàn) is a Chinese-origin noodle dish widely served in Singapore, most notably at hawker stalls such as Hock Kee Braised Beef Noodles (福记红烧牛肉面) at Lorong Telok. The dish consists of springy wheat noodles served in a deeply savoury, mahogany-coloured broth made by slow-braising beef shank with soy sauce, doubanjiang (chilli bean paste), star anise, cinnamon, ginger, and Shaoxing wine. The beef shank is braised until fork-tender, yielding a rich, gelatinous texture from the collagen in the shank. The broth is intensely umami, mildly spicy, and aromatic from the whole spices. Bok choy and scallions are added for freshness and a slight vegetal contrast. Nutritionally, a 600 g bowl provides approximately 650 kcal, 45 g protein, 25 g fat, and 60 g carbohydrate. Sodium is notably high at approximately 1800 mg per bowl, making portion control and broth avoidance important for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The estimated glycemic index of 52 (low-medium) is moderated by the substantial protein and fat content. The dish is not suitable for vegetarians, vegans, or those avoiding gluten or soy. It is a popular lunch and dinner option in Singapore's hawker culture.

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for braised beef noodles as a composite dish in the HPB Singapore Foodo database. Values are adapted from HPB component data and cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for beef shank and wheat noodles. The input record values (provenance: ai_generated, confidence 0.7) fall within the plausible range for this dish type and have been retained. GI is an analogous estimate based on boiled wheat noodles from the University of Sydney GI Database.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for this composite braised beef noodle dish is not available in the HPB Singapore Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central as a single composite entry as of 2024. potassium_mg set to -1: potassium data for this composite dish is not reported in the HPB Singapore database; estimated ~180 mg/100g from component ingredients (beef shank, bok choy, scallions) but not confirmed by an approved source and therefore not entered as a positive value. vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: micronutrient and moisture data for this composite dish are not available as a single entry in the HPB Singapore Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central as of 2024. Halal status is not confirmed; beef noodle dishes at Singapore hawker stalls may use non-halal beef and pork-derived ingredients in the broth. The dish contains gluten (wheat noodles) and soy (soy sauce, doubanjiang). Trans fat reported at 0.08 g/100g from the input record; this is a low but non-zero value consistent with trace trans fats in braised beef preparations.
